# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies with the biggest most unpredictable plot twists

**Score:** 29
**Body:** My boyfriends secret talent is predicting any movie plot twist. I want to put him to the test. Give me movies with the most unpredictable plot twists that are also a good watch. Please no spoilers either.

Some movies we’ve already watched:

\- shutter island

\- old boy

\- identity

\- incendies

\- calibre

\- Donnie Carlo

Ect ect

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Prestige. Don't even tell him that there is a twist. Memento might also be a good one. See if he can figure out how the movie comes to a middle. 
2. As always, Primal Fear.
3. The Crying Game 
4. "Sorry to Bother You" 
  - This right here. I’d be surprised to hear someone could figure out where this story ends up. 
5. Spoilers.  If you are expecting a plot twist, it will ruin it
6. sixth sense, the usual suspects, the skin i live in 
7. I read an essay on substack the other day that said "if anyone tells you that they figured out the (twist) in Arrival, theyre lying to you"
Also, a really good movie to boot.
8. Conclave
9. fallen and arlington road

## 2. Movies where men are kidnapped

**Score:** 19
**Body:** Movies where the main character is a man who gets kidnapped for any reason ransom, being in the wrong place at the wrong time, revenge, etc.

Edit-it should be kinda dark and after 2000s preferably 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Suicide Kings 1997 is a dark comedy with Christopher Walken.

Misery 1990 - is adapted from a Stephen King novel.

edit: fixed year
  - Misery - great shout as it's a great movie
2. Oldboy

I Saw the Devil
3. 9 to 5 (1980). A main character is kidnapped but it is more comedic. It is great movie. 
  - GREAT movie! 
4. Saw
5. Hostel and Saw
6. The King of Comedy (1982)
7. Pain and Gain 


Alpha Dog 








8. Audition (1999) Takashi Miike

## 3. Movies that helped with your health anxiety

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Anything that takes your mind away from it from those of you that have it or films that you relate to and felt it eased whatever sensations you were going through 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Little Miss Sunshine
2. I will add two that I think helped me not think about it. 

The Grand Budapest Hotel

Game Night. 

Both are fast pace and keep my mind active in better ways. 

Edit: silly misspelling
3. Good Will Hunting (1997)
4. Office Space. 
5. Tuesday (2023)

It might not be in the way you want it but it’s a beautiful movie with a sense of what life truly means in its basic sense. 

It calms me. An incredibly unique and underrated movie 
6. Brian and Charles.

Just feels like a mental hug every time I watch it. 
7. The Ballad of Wallis Island (2025)
8. Monty Python and the Holy Grail always takes my mind off of things. I still laugh my face off even though I’ve seen it dozens of times.
  - It's just excellent! I also watch Monty Python's Life of Brian several times.
9. I binge on Schitts Creek

## 4. Comedy movies like the nice guys, stupid, crazy love or snatch

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Im tryna find comedy movies that dont rely on on the nose humor, but more body language or clever dialogue, and that have an interesting plot or a specific good vibe. Pls suggest, I’ve watched snatch, the nice guys, crazy stupid love, bullet train, fargo (1996). Thanks!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Lock, Stock and Two Smoking Barrels 1998
2. Since you liked The Nice Guys:  
The Last Boy Scout  
The Long Kiss Goodnight  
Kiss Kiss Bang Bang (the best of these)  
Play Dirty

Since you liked Snatch:  
Lock Stock and Two Smoking Barrels  
The Gentlemen (the movie; I haven't seen the TV series)  
The Ministry of Ungentlemanly Warfare

Since you liked Bullet Train:  
The Fall Guy  
Nobody 1 and 2  
Deadpool 1-3

Since you liked Fargo:  
Raising Arizona  
The Hudsucker Proxy  
The Big Lebowski  
O Brother, Where Are Thou  
Intolerable Cruelty  
Burn After Reading  
Hail, Caesar!  
The Ballad of Buster Scruggs  
Fargo (the TV series; each season is a different story with a different cast)

Since you liked all of them:  
In Bruges  
Seven Psychopaths  
Midnight Run  
Mr. Right  
Game Night  
The Lovebirds  
Gangster Squad (has Ryan Gosling and Emma Stone)  
La La Land (has Ryan Gosling and Emma Stone)
3. Hot Fuzz
4. - The Gentleman 
- The Ministry of Ungentlemanly Warfare
- In Bruges
- Rock'n'rolla
- Seven Psychopaths 
- Zombieland
- Hitchhikers Guide to the Galaxy 
- Kick Ass
- Barry (not a movie but seriously funny and sort of fits the same genre)
  - Barry is great. NoHo Hank is one of, if not, the greatest character of all time.  This list is immaculate in respect to what OP is asking for. 
5. Kiss Kiss Bang Bang\
Tropic Thunder\
This Is The End\
Friendship\
What We Do In The Shadows\
Black Dynamite\
Thank You For Smoking\
Hit Man\
Hit Fuzz\
Knocked Up
6. Dogma.

Sort of a different vibe, and definitely has some on-the-nose humor…. but it’s packed with intelligent humor as well. 

If you were raised with any catholic background, it hits even harder. 
7. Silver Linings Playbook is sort of similar to Crazy Stupid Love in that it is an atypical Romcom. Jennifer Lawrence also won Best Actress for it.

I watched it for the first time the other day and I quite liked it.

For something else that fits the rest of your request you should really watch Burn After Reading. First half is the setup, second half is... well... just watch it.
8. Midnight Run
9. Lock, Stock, and Two Smoking Barrels

## 5. Movies that felt like a fever dream but in the best way possible?

**Score:** 15
**Body:** So I've been on a weird kick lately watching films that just leave you kinda disoriented after, like you're not sure what you just watched but you can't stop thinking about it. Just finished Annihilation recently and it scratched that itch perfectly, the kind of movie where the atmosphere does more talking than the actual plot.

Not looking for just confusing films, more like ones where the vibe, visuals, and story blend into something that feels... off in a good way. What are some movies like that you'd actually recommend? Bonus if it's something most people haven't seen.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Arguable as to whether it’s in a good way or not but since you mentioned Annihilation, I’ll throw out Midsommar.
2. Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind 
3. pretty much every gaspar noé film but especially climax and lux aeterna. 
4. [Dave Made A Maze (2017)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dave_Made_a_Maze) is a complete wtf fever dream from start to finish lol
5. Most people probably have seen them, but--

*Inception (2010)*

*Memento (2000)* -- Man with a  severe memory issue tries to find wife’s murderer. Film’s structure is unique: >!Main story scenes are shown in reverse *order*. Very disorienting for a reason, but it works.!<
6. Coherence 
7. Beau Is Afraid 
8. Color out of Space
9. Jacob's Ladder (1990)
10. Blue velvet

Mandy

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Marty supreme has lost all of its Oscar nominations

**Score:** 3194
**Body:** What do you think about that? Do you think it’s deserved? Is it surprising? Kind of ironic cuz it’s very Marty style lol

Honestly, I believe it should’ve won something. Maybe best actor or production design.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If Balls of Fury received no Oscar nominations, Marty Supreme deserved to lose all of its nominations. 
  - Balls of fury crawled so Marty supreme could also crawl 
    - Please, 

Christopher Walken killed it. Balls Of Fury strutted so Marty Supreme could crawl
2. All Safdie movies are about a guy running around frantically.
  - The Anxiety Trilogy
    - That’s pretty fucking accurate
3. no one cares about ping pong anymore
4. It was a good movie, but there were other good movies too so yeah, not everyone gets one. 
  - There are tons of really good movies who get spanked at the Oscars by great movies.  *Master and Commander* is a great movie but got spanked by the juggernaut *Return of the King* in 2004. 
    - Peter Jackson was like an emperor entering Rome.  Dude was gonna win. 

## 2. Jack o connell gave the best acting performance in Sinners imo. Crazy how he wasn't even nominated for the oscars .

**Score:** 2975

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Imagine if he had been, and then been the only award for Sinners.  The white guy in a black story.
  - I would, at that point, be convinced the academy was an agent of chaos working for someone trying to destabilize the United States 
2. If you haven't seen it, he is phenomenal in Starred Up.
  - And 28 Years Later - Bone Temple. 
    - Howzat
      - And god did nothing, for he was invisible… and cannae punch his way out of a paper fucking bag anyway. 
3. UK version of Skins has produced so many fantastic actors. 
  - "uk version"

I didn't even know there was a different skins 
    - The UK Skins is the original. 
4. I thought he crushed this and Bone Temple. Two banger Villains in one year.  

## 3. Sean Penn bags his third Oscar, two Best Actor awards and now Best Supporting Actor for One Battle After Another

**Score:** 1449

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. His first non-liquid Oscar.
2. I have a random memory of my second grade teacher just randomly stopping class one day in the middle of teaching a lesson and describing the plot to the movie mystic river (I learned this years later) and it still makes me crack up to this day he just wanted to talk about that movie with someone
  - Teachers like this truly change lives.

I had a teacher who was obsessed with a Bollywood film about cricket, and we always knew we could derail a class if we brought it up.
3. The scene in mystic river where he’s talking about his daughter on the porch is probably the best acting I’ve ever seen. 
  - there was a thread yesterday that some said he was overacting. 🤣  One of the few times an actor made me believe they really lost their child in a horrific way...excellent performance 
  - One shot Clint.
4. wild it was between 18 years 
5. He didn't get one for I am Sam. And we all know why.
6. Never got around to seeing Milk. Is it worth a watch?
7. Mystic river. Too powerful of an act. His character is scary and grieving at the same time.

## 4. The Oscar for “Best Picture” goes to One Battle After Another

**Score:** 1160

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Pat in "One Battle After Another" is like Indiana Jones in "Raiders of the Lost Ark": a lead character that conveys what the movie is all about despite not moving the plot forward

**Score:** 179
**Body:** So, by now, it's become a mainstream joke and/or consensus (even discussed in an episode of "The Big Bang Theory") that Indiana Jones plays no significant role in “Raiders of the Lost Ark”: the Nazis who were looking for the Ark would still have found it, would still have opened it, and would still have been killed if Indiana wasn't pursuing it too.

That’s the same point I sometimes see being raised about Pat, Leonardo DiCaprio’s character in “One Battle After Another”: he is always one step behind; he is a goof; his actions didn’t impact the outcome of the plot. (Some also see it as a stance the movie chose to take - the white (male) savior was unnecessary.) Yet I disagree with both interpretations. Because the outcome of a plot can't really convey what a movie is about.

The plot of "Raiders" is about getting to the Ark before the Nazis, yes. But the character who leads this mission is an academic who, in the early scenes, scorns whenever someone mentions a superstition or lore. But then, he comes out of this adventure believing there could be something beyond the physical world – that’s why he tells Marion, at the very end, to close her eyes when the Ark is opened. (The Nazis, who didn't close their eyes, are killed.)

So, the character of Indiana Jones is changed to his core because of what he discovered along the way - not just about this Ark, but by the knowledge shared by the people he met, the cultures he saw etc. And that’s exactly the same arc of Pat in “One Battle After Another”. He doesn’t arrive in time to save his daughter. The girl saves herself. But the point is he ARRIVES, and right when the girl is emotionally shaken by what she went through.

His ultimately purpose in this movie was simply to embrace his daughter, to give her a hug and some much needed comfort. That’s enough. But Pat only realized this because he had just went through his own journey and discovered a community and a support system - from his daughter’s Latino Sensei to some street skaters, they all helped him get there.

The character starts the movie as an overbearing, paranoid father, and ends the movie as someone who grew enough to trust his daughter and support her own political awakening by allowing her to go out, attend rallies, fight for the causes that are dear to her. That also ties back to the arc of Perfidia’s character, the girl's mother: she came from a family of revolutionaries and felt that succeeding at armed revolution was the one and only way to enact change.

Ultimately, the movie is about Willa’s development – she is neither sheltered nor indoctrinated. That’s, IMO, what the movie is about, way beyond the plot. And the message is only delivered because of Pat’s experiences and his inability to save the day, but still save his daughter from the traumatic emotional scars.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's because Bob isn't the protagonist. Willa is. PTA even made it a point to thank Chase at the end of the cast last night for playing the 'American Girl'.

As others have pointed out better than me, the movie owes more to T2 than Indiana Jones
  - >As others have pointed out better than me, the movie owes more to T2 than Indiana Jones

I haven't seen that take before but I'm definitely curious to read more about it
    - There's good stuff out there. I like this anecdote a lot:

"Anderson also enrolled at New York University’s film school, but rather than staying only two semesters, he stayed only two days. In the clip up top, from an interview with critic Elvis Mitchell, Anderson recounts the whole of his NYU experience. His first instructor announced, “If anyone is here to write Terminator 2, get out.” And so Anderson thought, “What if I do want to write Terminator 2? Terminator 2’s a pretty awesome movie.” (An assessment, incidentally, from which Wallace’s greatly differs.) When he turned in a page from a David Mamet script for his first assignment and his unsuspecting teacher gave it a C+, Anderson knew he had to leave. Living off of the tuition NYU returned to him, he got to work on a short film of his own."

https://www.openculture.com/2015/01/how-paul-thomas-anderson-dropped-out-of-nyu-film-school-in-2-days-studied-literature-with-david-foster-wallace.html#google_vignette
      - What NYU film professor wouldn’t recognise Mamet instantly. Especially in the 90s
2. I compare this movie regularly to modern Big Lebowski. I feel like Pat is more The Dude than Indiana Jones. The way he struggles to actually accomplish just about anything while still not stopping and continuing to push forward reminds me of the dude. I think the biggest negative to my theory is that pat is much more motivated to continue rather than being pulled back in by other parties midway through 
  - Also the bathrobe
  - I've been saying, the story is basically if you put The Dude in Taken
3. The difference is that the whole point of Raiders is to put Indy into action sequences. It's all built around action sequences. The plot isn't about the ark. That's a MacGuffin. The plot is about how to get Indy from one action scene to the next action scene. As long as he's after the ark, the movie works whether he changes the plot or not. The point is just having that goal.
4. Indy does lots in Raiders.  From fulfilling his mentor’s plans to fighting nazis to figuring out various puzzles.  He ultimately is willing to sacrifice everything for knowledge and ultimately survives b/c he knows seeing what is inside the Ark would be their undoing. 

Pat is a buffoon in the movie. Everyone does the hard work. Sensei.  Willa.  Lady Champagne.  They all are protagonists, while Pat is almost like Ernest going through the film. 

What he did do was raise a young girl to know the world is dangerous. He was a parent and provided a home.  He loved his daughter. 

It’s to juxtapose to Lockjaw, who never could love his daughter because of his own insecurities and hatred.  It’s to juxtapose to Prefrida who cannot give up her revolutionary identity.  

Anyway, it’s a family movie about kids learning lessons from parents.  Good or bad.  Willa has many people giving guidance beyond Pat and learns lessons good and bad. 
5. That Big Bang Theory myth about Indiana Jones is not true.

He was at the island at the end to take the Ark back to the Americans. If he wasn't there, other Nazis on the island would have taken it to to Berlin.

And Indy has agency throughout the movie and is one step ahead of the Germans for half of the film: he's the first one to get the Headpiece to the Staff of Ra, the first one to properly decode its message, the first one to get the Ark's proper location in the map room, and the first one to find the Ark. 

Your thoughts about "One Battle After Another" are reasonable enough, but they need a different analogy than Indy.

## 2. How much influence do Academy Awards still have on the long-term cultural relevance of films?

**Score:** 11
**Body:** Historically, the Academy Awards have played a major role in shaping how films are remembered. Winning Best Picture or major acting awards often helped elevate certain films into the cultural canon, while others faded despite critical acclaim.

However, the modern film landscape has changed significantly. Streaming platforms, online film communities, and algorithm-driven discovery now influence how audiences find and discuss movies. Because of this shift, I’m curious whether the Oscars still meaningfully shape which films become culturally significant over time.

Do Academy Award wins still help define the long-term reputation of films, or has their influence diminished as viewing habits and distribution models have evolved?

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The one area where it absolutely has an impact is that the academy is now regularly nominating 1-2 foreign language films in best picture every single year. That’s a massive boost to the exposure and relevance of those films in the US. A film like The Secret Agent might end up getting a million people streaming it or renting it on Netflix or Amazon or wherever. That exact same film, if it doesn’t get nominated for awards, will be lucky to sell 10k movie tickets in a few theaters in NY and LA and then go completely ignored and unnoticed in the void of streaming. 

I figure that applies to small American productions as well, ex: Blue Moon will likely have more cultural relevance than something like Me And Orson Welles or Everybody Wants Some (to name other Linklater films that made around the same amount of money) because it got Oscar nominations and those others didn’t. In 20 years, a teenager/young adult will discover Richard Linklater, and they’ll use the Oscar’s history to figure out that Blue Moon, and the Before trilogy, and Boyhood should probably be the first ones to dive into. 

Does it scale up to the level of mainstream cultural relevance of some winners of the past? I don’t think so, but movies as a whole aren’t on the same level of cultural relevance that they were in the past. The death of the monoculture, movement away from reruns on cable TV, a general vibe shift to shorter content, whatever reason you want to pin it on, it exists. 
  - I meet someone form my country who worked with a director who win one oscar and was nominated twice and you are right, but is more about our own countries than u.s. This person find a stable job in a university after working in that projects and get money from the state and some sponsors to keep going with theirnprwctice. Film in some countries is hard AF, we live in a place were the people and our politicians thinks this kind of jobs are for lazy people who don't want to work
2. I'm a believer that the cream rises to the top. History shows that plenty of movies which won Oscars have been relegated to side-screens whereas their competition has been immortalized as cultural touchstones of their era. 

So generally I think that the Oscars are a fun show and a diversion for us movie fans and I think of them more as an opportunity to celebrate films that may have been forgotten amid blockbuster releases. 
3. I feel like the Oscars might somehow be becoming an even stronger pop culture force, frustratingly so. This awards season had the very strange, almost stan-war divide between One Battle After Another and Sinners that I had never really seen before, where it seemed like people online had to pick one and defend it, while trashing the other, also Marty Supreme mostly going award-less seems to have become a stick that people that didn't like it (or don't like Timothee Chalamet) use to beat it with.

There seems to be an attitude that the film that wins the most oscars essentially "wins" that year in film. This turns the year into a sort of long-haul competition, like seasons in sports. One film is crowned champion at the end and all the others basically lost.

This sports mentality in mainstream film discourse really stuck out to me this year. Winners and losers, fans devoting themselves to one film like it's their team, taunting each other online, etc... What really bothers me is that this frames the efforts of the people making films as being merely attempts at the trophy, and anything short of it is a failure. This is mostly the impression I got from mainstream audiences on tiktok and instagram, I don't think people that actually are into film think like this.
  - Its kind of always been like this, but in the past there wasnt an outlet for people to voice their opinions. You used to just get into arguments with people iro at the oscar party, then it was over. but now people spend 8 months arguing online. It sucks. 
  - It is extra odd to apply a sports mentality to film awards because most competitive sports have somewhat objective criteria to decide who wins. The Oscars are just voted on by a related but somewhat arbitrary group of people, who may not even watch all the nominees before voting.
    - I literally saw someone argue just earlier today that OBAA was "objectively" better than Bugonia because it won Oscars.
      - “Objectively” is this generation‘s “literally”. They don’t know what it means. 
      - A lot of people seem to think that aggregating subjective opinions leads gives an objective result. I guess they have a fundamental misunderstanding of what that all means.
  - You're definitely right and I think there are a few reasons for this. I mean the rise of letterboxd has been overall a good thing imo but it has also led (due to people having a unique profile and a top 4) to people really attaching themselves personally to films. It's not just that you like a film, now you use the films you like as a very public display of your personality and taste.

I also think with Sinners this is the first time in a while that there has been an Oscar race where one of the movies involved has broad mainstream appeal. (Oppenheimer was of course very popular but there wasn't really a race that year - it just swept). So more people than usual are invested in who wins what and it's a different type (more casual moviegoer) of person involved in the conversation as well.

## 3. What are the best English language drama films (including melodrama, crime, Western and epic) between 1950-1969 in your opinion? Alternatively, what’s a film from this time that deserves more attention than it gets?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** This is a simple post. Inspired by the post [https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/comments/1rvdu3q/how\_much\_influence\_do\_academy\_awards\_still\_have/](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/comments/1rvdu3q/how_much_influence_do_academy_awards_still_have/) asking what the influence of a Best Picture nomination is upon a film‘s long term relevance, I want to ask this sub to **name between one and ten** of what you consider the best Hollywood or British films from the 1950s and 1960s.

In this category of English language films of the fifties and sixties I personally find that the list of Best Picture nominees is the best resource to get a recommendation for what to watch.

Comments in the other post dispute that the Best Picture nominees represent the best remembered films of their years. I won’t argue with that. But, as I said, looking at the Best Picture nominees of this particular era has been very satisfactory to me so far.

I don’t favor any particular genre, except I suppose the broad category of drama. I think I have a lot of tolerance for what will seem like datedness and ponderousness of ideas in typical prestige films of past decades. *North by Northwest* was cited in the other thread as a classic overlooked by the Oscars of its time, only to today reputationally tower above the nominees of its year. To me, it isn’t such an interesting film.

I’m always eager for a good discussion and recommendation. I’d really appreciate if you named a handful of what you think are the best English language films of the 50s and 60 and maybe say why they are for you the best.

Per a list I once made, my favorites from this period are:

*A Place in the Sun*

*Some Like it Hot*

*Sunset Boulevard*

*Vertigo*

*Psycho*

*The Searchers*

*2001: A Space Odyssey*

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I would like to mention Whose afraid of Virginia Woolf, I just saw it for the first time the other day and it's really a masterpiece in building tension and layering character intents and meaning, It was also pretty groundbreaking for the time 
2. The Wild Bunch (1969)

The Swimmer (1968)

The Graduate (1967)

Breakfast at Tiffany’s (1961) (if that counts as drama)

Night of the Hunter (1955)

12 Angry Men (1957)

Are among my favorite dramas per your definition from that time span. Several obvious choices and I lean towards the latter films of that timespan as we near the 70s. 

Several Hitchcock films don’t vibe for me as much as they do for others like To Catch A Thief and North By Northwest. I don’t think that means they get undue attention but aren’t always quite my taste. 
  - I had a different text written in the body of the post which I lost went I went to get the link to the Oscars post, and in that text I went into saying, I’m not necessarily looking for suggestions of musicals, nor screwball comedies (but kind of a moot point by this era, I think), so freely suggest comedies.

Anyway, I love the film *Breakfast at Tiffany’s*. I want to give it another watch to really know my thoughts on it. I’d almost call it a favorite, but I would be prevented if by nothing else, by the Mickey Rooney character. I think Capote is fantastic. Beat the Devil and The Innocents, each of which he wrote the screenplay for, is great. I want to rewatch In Cold Blood some time as well. I didn’t like it too much outside of the cinematography.
3. For starters, I'd say *Wait Until Dark*. Criminally underrated crime/suspense film. Even if AH won the Best Actress Oscar for it, this movie still deserves much more attention.

Also, *The Night of the Hunter*. Americana noir masterpiece.
  - Thanks for the suggestions. I only remember seeing a small part of *Wait Until Dark* as a child.
4. Off the top of my head and in no particular order:

+ **The Lion in Winter** (1968) - An absolute masterclass in acting with truly one of the greatest scripts ever written. If you've never seen it, the best way I can think to describe it is like Shakespeare meets Game of Thrones meets Succession. Katharine Hepburn gives a performance for the ages. 
+ **Marty** (1955) - This is one of the lesser known Best Picture Winners, but at a tight c. 90 minutes, it's a lovely, melancholic little film depicting loneliness and the desperate desire to find companionship whilst being held back by fear of never finding it. It really moved me. 
+ **The Sound of Music** (1965) - This is a childhood favourite of mine. Everything about it - from the sweeping cinematography, to the production design, to the music - is perfect. 
+ **Summertime** (1955) -  A companion piece to Marty, this is another film about loneliness and finding love slightly later in life. It's directed by David Lean, who is of course better known for his grand epics, but this is more similar to something like *Before Sunrise*. It also features another wonderful performance from Katharine Hepburn. 
+ **Sunset Boulevard** (1950) - I know *Double Indemnity* has its fans, but to me this is Billy Wilder's noir masterpiece. 
+ **The Apartment** (1960) - One of the greatest rom-coms every made, featuring another excellent script from Billy Wilder. Carefully balances the darkness and humour, and feels timeless despite being nearly 70 years old. 
+ **The Night of the Hunter** (1955) - Deeply underappreciated in its time, this remains one of the most visually-fascinating and deeply creepy films I've seen from this era. Robert Mitchum plays an iconic villain. 
+ **Rear Window** (1954) - What even needs to be said about this one? To me, it's Hitchcock's masterpiece. It's the film (along with 12 Angry Men) I first recommend to people looking to get into "old" cinema. 
+ **12 Angry Men** (1957) - Simply one of the greatest films ever made. 
+ **Judgment at Nuremberg** (1961) - An acting masterclass from some of the biggest heavy-hitters of the era. It is devastating at times, and remains horrifically relevant to the times we're now living in. 
  - I watched two Stanley Kramer films in the last couple of nights (*Not As a Stranger* and *On the Beach*). I thought they were really enjoyable. That’s informing some of what I wrote above. I still have *Inherit the Wind* and *Judgment at Nuremberg* to watch, and others.

The films of this era dealing with the British crown are also a blind spot for me still. *A Man for All Seasons* and *Beckett* as well as *A Lion in Winter* and some others. I do mean to get to them, maybe first by tackling Zinnemann in the way I’m going through Kramer now, and on from there. I’d also begun to go through Joseph Mankiewicz’s films, and am into Katherine Hepburn much more than before because of *Suddenly, Last Summer* and *Guess Who’s Coming to Dinner*.

I think I saw a trailer from Criterion Channel which was doing a Venice films program and that was where I first saw anything of *Summertime*. It looks like a visually brilliant film, and very under the radar until recently, I believe.
5. Movies that have left the deepest impressions on me from this era are from the great American playwrights.

A Long Days Journey into Night (1962 version with Katherine Hepburn) - First viewing literally changed my life and got me into recovery.

A Streetcar named Desire (1951)

Cat on a Hot Tin Roof (1958)

Suddenly Last Summer (1959)

Picnic (1955 - Kim Novak was breathtaking)

Who's afraid of Virginia Woolf (1966)

The Long Hot Summer (1958) - not a play though based on Faulkner, but unbelievable performances by Paul Newman and Joanne Woodward.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I agree, I Lied to You is an incredible *scene*, but listening to it just as a song isolated from the picture, it's not nearly as much of a standout. Gotta figure that they assumed the quality of the scene would influence votes regardless. 
  - I mean, in context of the Academy Awards, that is exactly why it should have won the Oscar over Golden.

Shouldn't songs be inextricably a part of the stories they are telling?

I Lied to You, as a stand alone song, really doesn't make a lot of sense. But that shouldn't really be the standard in which we view film songs.
    - Taste is subjective, but I'm pretty sure people will be using Golden as a key example of music theater "I Wish" song writting for years to come.  It drives the plot AND supports the worldbuilding, AND serves as the emotional mission statement for Rumi (while also managing to tell the audience a little more about who Zoey and Mira are).  And it does this very specific work in the film while also being widely relatable as a pop song.

The production is a good as anything in K-pop, which is to say as good as or better than anything else in the world.  It's constantly varied throughout song, yet is always emotionally precise. It has the hooks, but also ties them to feelings. The music is used to do the "classic Hollywood thing", and re-appears as leitmotif. 

In terms of performance, you can easily spend a \*lot\* of time on YouTube watching reaction videos from vocal coaches. The song's three-octave range and operatic A5 high note aren't just stunts; they underpin what the movie is is telling us about how popular Huntrix is, and also selling the idea that the characters have supernatural abilities.

In terms of audience reaction, and global reach, obviously there is no comparison.  In a few years, the main discourse about nearly all of the nominated films will be movie geeks like us asking "when did the blu ray for that modern classic go out of print, and how can I get a copy?"   Decades from now, people will hear Golden playing at some random grocery store and feel things.   

It dominated the charts in the way that musical theater stuff never does in this century.  It achieved what Hamilton never did: a #1 spot on the Billboard Hot 100.  It rose faster and further than "Let it Go" from Frozen.   

And while some might object that "being popular" isn't necessarily an indicator of quality, in this case, it's literally part of the the nearly impossible assignment that the song pulls off.  "Golden" is as popular in real life as the Huntrix song is said to be in the movie.

In any almost any other year, I'd probably have been rooting for "I Lied to You".  It's definitely doing more work in the film than the typical nominee--which are often just undistinguished minor songs performed by noted artists, vaguely on a related theme. 

But "Golden" as a once in a generation near \*perfect\* I Wish/I Want song that this the backbone of narrative, that \*also\* happens to be a chart topping hit, which is what is exactly what it song supposed to be in the context of the story being told.  

It's rather silly say that Golden isn't essential to the story K-Pop Demons Hunters is telling.
2. Nothing would’ve beat Golden. That was the biggest song of last year period, beyond movies and soundtracks. Borderline unfair having it compete. I Lied To You was the big showcase song in the middle of the movie and probably its best bet. And it’s live performance tonight put the Golden performance to shame. Just hard to compete with Golden when it comes to the voters. 
3. I Lied to You have *the* sequence of the film. Sometimes they like a song that has a thematic relevance to the film more than the song itself. ie Barbie for I’m just Ken vs the Billie Eilish song
  - Considering the history of the category, it makes sense. It was original created in the era of musicals, with the presumption that the nominees would be songs that were pivotal to the storytelling and an integrated part of the movie, as opposed to just something that plays over the end credits or gets slapped onto a montage.
